speaker: interviewer
text: Who have you brought in, Mr. Baer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I followed the Biblical-- you see, you see, I am constantly interrupted. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you interviewing her, or me? She's next week.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, you said your piece. I want equal time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, Mr. Baer, I think we'll be able to hear, in spite of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will try to make myself heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Equal time. I have the great pleasure and the good fortune of being surrounded by the people I love most in life. Here to my left is my Birgitta, who has given me a wonderful new home. And certainly my children. To my right is my youngest daughter, Leslie, and her little daughter, who loves to make herself heard. I have a feeling she won't be bashful later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julia. And next to my wife is Gabi-- Gabriella-- my eldest daughter, with her husband. And Leslie's husband is Victor, who's behind us. And these are my daughters and one of my grandchildren. The others are absent, but certainly, in spirit, I'm sure they are with us and they would say their piece very eloquently if they were here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Gabi's husband's name is-- I think you've mentioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eli-- Eliyahu. And Leslie's husband's name is Victor. And this is Julia. She's our spokesperson right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if I-- I can direct a question to Julia, then. [LAUGHTER] As a family, is it something-- the the experiences that your-- your father or father-in-law had, or husband had-- is it something that you grew up being aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want me to answer that?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you feel comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it talked about a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, my mother-- well-- and my mother and grandmother would tell about their relatives, you know, who were killed in the Holocaust, and the experiences that they had in Nazi Germany, and the people who were formerly, supposedly, friends, turning away. Also the few people who showed them kindnesses and saved lives, too, and then their losses, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of special-- if any, what kind of special senses of the world has this given you, this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for me, it's really-- I grew up realizing that, in many respects, I was very different from my friends who-- even the friends of mine who were Jewish, most of them did not have families with the same kind of history. And, in a way, it gives you a little bit more insecure feeling about the world. But on the other hand, to me it's also very inspiring. Because if my parents can come to a country under the worst of circumstances and make a life for themselves that is meaningful and -- and live again, despite what they went through, that's very inspiring for me, when I go through good times and bad. And that's been, I think, a great gift, as well as-- not really a burden, but as well as something difficult to bear that is not forgettable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Victor and Eli, does it create any kind of special connection for you, in any kind of way? Or is it not an issue, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I came from Israel, so the whole Shoah story is not new to me. I mean, I was aware of it all my life, practically. And the stories that my father-in-law told us about his experience in South America and in Germany-- in some sense, I was aware of it and familiar with that kind of story, so to me it was not new. But, on the other hand, living with his family, I always admired him for having gone through that experience and be able to cope with it. And, as, again, my sister-in-law said, started a new life and raising a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly for me it's also very, very inspirational. I have a lot of the same feelings expressed. It has really shown me that, throughout adversity, that you can overcome adversity in a tough situation, the toughest circumstances, and you can't let that stop you. It is certainly a life lesson for me. And I, too, have heard about the Holocaust and have listened to John talk about it. And Leslie has told me the things that she learned when she was growing up. And it's a legacy-- something I want to pass on to my daughter, certainly. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And is it something that you were at all familiar with, Birgitta, before you joined the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. I was just a small child when the war was over, living in my native Sweden. And I was there as a child. I was very much protected from some of the-- some of the more gruesome facts. But, we-- of course, we were living in-- in fear of the Germans. This one I was very much aware of, even though my parents tried to, you know, protect me from this sort of thing. I became aware of the particulars, of the horror stories, as a as a child, right after the war. I was rummaging through an attic of my uncle's, which I loved to do, and came across some old type thing with stories of the concentration camps. And that was my first detailed encounter with it. And I could only have been about 10 or something like that. And it was really unforgettable. And since I came-- since I came to America, of course I have encountered many more Jewish people. Because Sweden had a relatively small percentage of Jewish population. And I would say I have waited a long time for the perfect husband, before finally finding him. And, for me, it has been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is he? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And, for me, it's been it's been wonderful to get a new and very big family, here in this country, while the family I have in Sweden are naturally there and very close my heart. But they are also physically distant. So this has been a very, very positive experience. And I admire very much John's positivism and his strength-- how someone with his experiences can be so emotionally whole, as he is. Which I must ascribe to what I think a wonderful first wife and a very long and happy marriage must have been a very stabilizing influence. And also, naturally, to his own spirituality-- and desire for spirituality.

speaker: interviewee
text: You left out one person who was helping me, other than my children-- and you. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I also want to ask Birgitta and Victor-- as non-Jews coming into the family, did-- did this connect with certain aspects of your own experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll let Victor answer first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, certainly for me. Knowing what my own family has gone through in the United States, certainly-- of course, the Holocaust-- I mean, a major-- the history of mankind. But certainly, the sense of struggle. I think that one can go in a direction of just letting it overwhelm you, or one can learn from the experience and take something positive from it and learn from it. So I myself, actually I converted to Judaism. And I am very proud of being a convert and practicing Judaism. And we are raising our daughter in  a Jewish home. And I think that's a testament to just learning and studying and listening to what John and others have gone through, in order to make it in this country, and just to make it in this world and have a life. It's something that-- that I think about. And, again, to just trying to learn and to bring something positive and think about the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me it's been a very enriching experience. I came to this family circle later in life than than than Victor did. And I had been struck, and had been struck already before I met John, how many similar family values there were among the Jewish people that I met here and among the Swedes and the Europeans, in that sense. We stuck together, family meetings, celebrating holidays, birthdays, and things together-- things that that I had- I had really missed in that sense. Religiously, we respect one another very much. We follow one another to the different holidays, within our different religions. And I think both John and I have been struck by the fact that this has not provided a-- a different-- a separation between us, because there was so much more in our background than I think we had imagined that was similar. Because we came from relatively well-to-do, bourgeois families in Northern Europe-- similar climates, similar periods, similar social values-- food one ate, things you did, things of that sort-- which, I think, continues to surprise us, how much we have in common, in that sense. That has been a bond, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very interesting. Very interesting. I have no further questions. Is there any comment anyone would like to make about this experience that I haven't touched upon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd just like to say that, I mean, we are particularly-- we're very, very glad to be doing this. At least for Victor and me, this-- and I also-- I think I'm more-- I'm trying to speak for both of us, but-- Having had Julia has been a whole new idea for me, in terms of what I wish to pass on. And I never really had to think about it, because I went through 36 years of my life without having a child. And it became all of the-- the theory became very real, once we had her, and all of the ideas about planning out and what  we'd we want her to remember. And I think one of the things for us that's so wonderful about this whole project is that this is something that we can pass on to her, and that she can look at and she can learn from and understand who her family is and where they come from. And that's something that's very important for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have to say I really admire Mr. Spielberg for what he's doing. I want to get that in, because I think that's-- that's great, that he is using his talents, his skill, and his financial resources to something that's very important to the Jewish people. Because I think this could be forgotten easily, you know? And I've tried to have my children know about it and have them talk to my father about it. And when my mother was alive and my grandmother, they they talked about their experiences. But I think a lot of people will not know-- would not know anything about it unless someone with that kind of stature as Mr. Spielberg did that kind of a project.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gabi, don't you also-- Gabi belongs to the second generation to a group. You might want to talk about that-- Leslie and both-both of you-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

words.text: that-- Leslie and both-both of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think Le-- yeah, Leslie-- is-- is, um-- Leslie is the one who-- she belongs to that-- I mean, there's-- I think that's-- also, the people who are second generation of the survivors, I guess, who are getting together and trying to see what they have in common. Try to continue-- try to support efforts to keep the history of the Holocaust alive, which is-- One of Leslie's very close friends is president of that group. They're very active.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's, I think, so important is at the personal level from my parents-- it's something that was a part of my life, from a very, very young age. And it's wonderful and it's difficult. And it's it's very inspiring to have parents who have been able to live through what they have. And it is very important, I think, to not just recall the the misery and how difficult it was, but to realize that we have come out of it, and to use the history for the future, to not use it just as a negative, but to you use it as a positive. And I think that's what's so wonderful about project and about the 2G group, as well-- that we're trying use things as something inspirational and something positive to pass on.

speaker: interviewee
text: To keep their traditions alive. I try to do that with my my children. The continuity of Judaism, and not relying on someone else to do  it for you. You have to do it yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish traditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish tradition. You have to do. But, I mean, you know, keep the ritual alive, not, you know,  just say, well, I'm I'm a Jew in my heart. That doesn't transmit to the next generation. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and the meaning of the ritual is so important. I know Julia, when we first brought her home from the hospital, we had a family Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the first Shabbat we-- remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came over, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the reasons that it's so important for us is  it's something I told Victor-- he and I have been married 13 years, now. It's something that was so important to me. Growing up, some of the most beautiful memories I have were lighting the Shabbat candles. And that's something that we want to pass on to Julia. And that was terrific, because everybody came over that Friday night, and we had Shabbat for the first time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had that in my family, and when Ursula was alive, the family came to our house. And we were all one in the welcoming Shabbat and lighting the candles.

speaker: interviewer
text: It occurs to me that Julia's about a year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a year old next month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she never got to meet her great-grandmother-- her grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, unfortunately not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that Ursula's involved in her life, just the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very much so. I have a lot of wonderful stories and memories. And the great gift of a fantastic relationship with my mother, who was not just a mother but, as I got older, one of my dearest friends and someone I could-- I trusted. And Victor- she and Victor became very, very close, too. And if I can have half the relationship with Julia as I had with my mother, it would be a great gift. And that's something I strive to do and to tell her about her grandmother and all the wonderful things we did together-- things that she thought. I'd like to pass that on.

speaker: interviewee
text: One other aspect that-- you know, with Leslie and I and our differences in background, something that's real interesting is that in some African tribes they believe that the spirit skips every generation. So, oftentimes, when we're with Julia, we look at her, and we think, there's some things of Ursula that are there. And so certainly there in spirit. And something that we think about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of her facial expressions, some of her-- the way she looks, sometimes-- just a blank. And it's- it's- What's beautiful also with Julia is that she has cousins who've been waiting a long time [LAUGHS] for us to have a baby!

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my daughters used to go--

speaker: interviewee
text: And who are crazy about her, too. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They used to go to my sister and go shopping with her, and go like, tick, tock, tick tock. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for telling that that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were pretty blunt about this. You know, they were not diplomatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, great-- sure..

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just want to also say that this family certainly is loving and welcoming, in terms of me, you know, converting. And I've really appreciated along the way the things that I've been show about a real Jewish home and about Judaism and some of the great things. Certainly, coming out of the Holocaust I think one would have thought, you know, oh, gosh, you know, this is just this is certain event has happened, and-- but there are some good things, too. And that's special.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of those things that you are happy was not killed off in the Holocaust? In other words, that you can survive and pass on to your children. What are the positive things that you want to pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, certainly-- I mean, the essence of Judaism itself. The basic essence of Judaism, to me, is that belief of self-responsibility. And, yes, questioning of the world, but also questioning of yourself and challenging yourself. And that's something that's certainly survived the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: In talking about the survivors and the passing on of the memory of this, along the lines of Mr. Spielberg's efforts. I would also-- I would also like to say that I think that the memoirs that John had just finished are a very important part of this effort. It is one puzzle piece in a much larger picture, where many persons in your situation are doing this. And this, I think, is is is very important. Everybody has a little piece of the picture, and and it flows into a similar stream for others to read and to relate to and to learn from, later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Percipient witnesses should bear testimony as long as they're alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's what the Shoah project is about. And it's my privilege, as one of the representatives here of the Shoah Foundation, to thank you very much, each of you-- and, of course, to you, Mr. Baer, for participating in this project.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for giving us that opportunity. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there anything about the-- you asked me to bring pictures of the children. Is there anything about-- my Dad asked me to bring pictures of my children. I don't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: We can do that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that something--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the next step.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the next step? Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why don't we stop here and go ahead and look at some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because we have--

speaker: interviewee
text: These are photos of my parents, photos taken in Breslau, Germany. On the left side is my dad. I believe that was taken in 1937 or '38. On the right side is my mother. I believe that was taken-- she was wearing black. It was while she was mourning my father's death. Must have been late '38, early '39, in Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us their full names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Bernhard-- B-E-R-N-H-A-R-D-- Baer. My mother's name was Marta-- M-A-R-T-A-- Baer, maiden name Block. And her Jewish name was Miriam. My father's Jewish name was Benjamin. This is a group meeting of the Betar-- Brit Trumpeldor-- the revisionist youth group, in Germany. It was, I think, in nineteen-hundred and late '36 or early '37. We met in the city of Beuthen-- B-E-U-T-H-E-N-- in Upper Silesia. I recognize quite a few of these people, here, who were present. Some of them later on went to the United States. You see myself, there, and a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Under the arrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the arrow. On the right-hand side, you see in the background, there is a picture of Yosef Trumpeldor, who died in Tel Hai in 1920 and who was our-- if you will-- our role model, our hero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he in the white shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he wearing the white shirt-- in the back row?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know who that is. No. But I'm talking about that central picture, where all you see is a cap-- in the middle. And that is Joseph Trumpeldor. And, underneath, there was, in Hebrew, a sign 'Tov lamut be'ad artzenu.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means? 'It is good to die'--

speaker: interviewee
text: 'It is good to die for our country.'

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a snapshot of Ursula and myself, at the Malibu Beach. It was taken around 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be in Los Angeles, California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Malibu, California. This is a wedding picture. At the Beverly Hilton Hotel, Beverly Hills, California. The wedding of my daughter Gabi to Eli, who's on her right. And that was, I think, the 31st of January, 1960. To our extreme right is my father-in-law, George Boehm, my mother-in-law Edith Boehm, and then, skipping the bride and the bridegroom, Ursula is next to the bride, on her left side, and I am on the extreme left. This picture was taken in 1980, on the occasion of my receiving the French Declaration from the president of France, in Beverly Hills. To my extreme-- to my right is my youngest daughter, Leslie. To my left is my wife, Ursula. And to her left is her mother, Edith Boehm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually it would be to the left of the picture. To the right of the picture is your daughter, and then on the left side of the picture is your wife and mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and my mother-in-law, Edith Boehm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are for decorations which I received from four European governments which I represented as a lawyer, here, starting with the one on the upper left side. This is Germany. I'm sorry, this is Austria. And on the right side, Germany. And here my services also involved my desire to build bridges between the second generations of Austria and the second Jewish generation here. And the Austrian decoration was given me by the then-counsel general Thomas, Klestil, who is today the president of Austria. And still a very dear friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, on the bottom row?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom-- the bottom ones are-- this is the highest civilian French decoration, which was given me by Francois Mitterrand. He was president of the French Republic. For also professional services to the French and also helping them establish commercial ties and cultural ties with Southern California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the blue?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one, here, the one with the white cross, is the government of Italy. And with the green ribbon. That's the government of Italy, which I assisted here as a lawyer and also in connection with the establishment of commercial ties and ties from people to people. Because I was also involved in the sister-city program here between Los Angeles and these respective countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the green one is from Italy, and the blue one is from France.

speaker: interviewee
text: The green one is from Italy. The blue one is from France. Viewing this picture fills me with deep melancholy. It was taken on the 3rd of December, 1988, on the occasion of our invitation by the board of supervisors of the county of LA to a dinner-- I think Christmas dinner-- in the Museum of Natural History. It was the last time that Ursula and I went to a function, a public function, before she passed on, less than a year after that. And it is also one that I cherish more than everything else-- anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in Los Angeles, California?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Los Angeles, in the museum. These are my three grandchildren by my daughter Gabi. On the left is Tamar, the youngest. In the middle is Danny, the oldest. And on the right side is Michal, who is in between the two. And that was taken on the occasion of Michal's wedding, on August 8, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles--

speaker: interviewer
text: California.

speaker: interviewee
text: California. This is my sister, Lily Glucksmann, taken in Melbourne, Australia, where she resides. The picture was taken in 1994. Her son, Michael, who started out as a chazzan for one of the Melbourne synagogues, is now an opera singer-- soprano. And he sings, among other things, Tevye in and also Wagner. He plays Hans Sachs in Wagner's
